title: The Macro Minute: Are the US and Europe breaking up?

skipper here at Darius Dell to present
our macro minute for Wednesday January
21st 2026. As always we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. So let's dive
right in. Today's key micro question is
are the US and Europe breaking up? The
short answer is probably not yet. But
the risk of China swooping in with a
better deal to woo Europe toward its
pole in the new multipolar world order
is not inconsequential, especially in
the context of the growing
geopolitically driven supply demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market
that Ray Dallio and myself continue to
warn about. In case you missed it, don't
miss his commentary uh with CNBC at the
World Economic Forum, which I hyperl in
today's leadup morning note uh and and
posted on uh Twitter and LinkedIn. Uh,
terms of analytical nuance, addressing
many of the world's most important
business and political leaders at the
World Economic Forum in Davos on
Wednesday, President Trump called for
immediate discussions aimed at the US
acquiring Greenland, framing the Danish
territory as critical to the collective
security while minimizing any
prospective threat to NATO. He escalated
pressure on Europe by warning that
failure to relinquish control of
Greenland could carry consequences,
asserting that NATO was obligated to
grant the US full rights to the Arctic
territory. He emphasized that the
island's status as a sovereign Danish
territory does not override US national
security interests. He also
characterized the proposal as a small
ask relative to decades of US defense
support for NATO allies. In an interview
with RTO radio on Wednesday, a very
frustrated ECB President Christine
Lagarde, who walked out on US Commerce
Secretary Howard Lutnik's incendiary
speech last night, warned that quote,
"We are seeing the curtain come up on a
new world order." She added, "Threating
to take a territory like Greenland that
is not for sale and brandishing tariffs
and restrictions on global trade isn't
really behaving like an ally. The new
world order must lead us to deeply
revise the way we organize our economy
in Europe in the way we build ties with
other countries perhaps like China in
the world who play by the same rules as
us. Although the sell America trade is
not on in the macro sense today,
investors must be aware that small caps
outperform the broader US equity market
for a 13th consecutive session. Our
small cap mega cap ratio has been
overwhelmingly bullish from the
perspective of of our volatility just to
momentum signal since December 4th. our
magnificent 7 MCI all country world
index ratio recently broke down to
neutral VAMS on January 14th and our
tech and communication services market
cap uh to rate S to total S&P 500 market
cap ratio broke down to neutral VAMS on
January 14th as well. We continue to
believe that diversifying out of what
our foreign creditors are overallocated
to is the correct riskmanagement pivot
for anyone getting compensated by
clients to remain on the right side of
factories. This is one of the reasons
we've long preferred broad US equity
beta in KISS as opposed to concentrating
our exposure to productivity growth in
the NASDAQ 100 or worse from a risk
management perspective, the Magnificent
7. Prior January 3rd around the horn.
Investors must commit to disciplined
datadriven risk management to maximize
upside capture amid growing quantities
of monetary debasement and financial
repression and minimize downside capture
during the coming secular bare market
that is highly likely to follow the AI
capex bubble just as there was after the
railroad consumer durable goods and
internet capex bubbles. Reminder, the
panic of 1873 led to the long
depression. The 1929 stock market crash
led to the great depression and the
do-com bus uh led to the early 2000's
jobless recovery and housing bubble
which gave way to the global financial
crisis. The S&P 500 declined 86% and
took 25 years to recover its September
20 1929 high due to the 1929 stock
market crash. The NASDAQ 100 declined
83% and took 16 years to recover its
March 2000 high due to the dotcom bust.
The S&P 500 declined 57% and took six
years to recover its October 2007 high
due to the global financial crisis. As
always, members of our global investor
community can trust that our
institutional-grade risk management
overlays. Kiss and Dr. Mo will help our
portfolios navigate these emergent risks
better than investors attempting to
manage risk without systematic rules or
relying on fundamental research alone.
